questionText: Scores, $X$, on a standardised aptitude test in a large population are modelled by $X \sim N(100, 15^2)$. Find the score that is exceeded by only $25\%$ of the population (the upper quartile), giving your answer to $3$ significant figures.

steps.description: State the distribution and the condition
steps.explanation: Scores are normal with mean $\mu = 100$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 15$. The upper quartile $a$ is the score exceeded by the top $25\%$, so $P(X > a) = 0.25$.
The inverse-normal reads areas to the left, so we rewrite: if $25\%$ of scores exceed $a$, then $75\%$ fall below it, giving $P(X < a) = 0.75$.
The standardised value with $75\%$ of the area to its left is $z = 0.6745$. It is positive because the upper quartile sits above the mean.
Standardising the unknown score $a$ must reproduce this $z$-value, so we equate the two expressions.
Multiplying both sides by $\sigma$ and adding $\mu$ makes $a$ the subject — the general rule for turning a $z$-value back into a score.
Putting in the mean $100$, the standard deviation $15$ and the $z$-value $0.6745$ gives the score in the original units.
The arithmetic gives a score of about $110.12$.

steps.description: State the answer to 3 significant figures
steps.explanation: Rounding gives the upper quartile — the score exceeded by only the top $25\%$ of the population.
The score lies above the mean of $100$, which is exactly right for an upper quartile: three quarters of the population score below it and only a quarter score above.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $a \approx 110$ (3 s.f.) — the upper quartile score, exceeded by $25\%$ of the population.
